content:
# Matthew Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 4 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 4:1 — "tempted" vs. "tested" for Greek πειρασθῆναι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "tempted" | Standard rendering of πειράζω in context of the devil |
| LOGOS | "tempted" | Same — adversarial context favors "tempted" |
| PNEUMA | "tested" | Emphasizes the Spirit's initiative — the purpose is divine testing, not merely Satanic enticement |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "tempted" — while PNEUMA correctly notes that πειράζω encompasses both "tempt" and "test," the immediate agent is named as "the devil" (τοῦ διαβόλου), making "tempted" the contextually appropriate choice. The Spirit's testing purpose is implicit in the narrative structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. The adversary's role determines the word choice.

---

## 4:2 — "he was hungry" vs. "he hungered" vs. "he was famished"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he hungered" | Verbal form matching Greek ἐπείνασεν |
| LOGOS | "he was hungry" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "he was famished" | Intensified — 40 days of fasting warrants stronger language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he was hungry" — the Greek ἐπείνασεν is simply "he hungered/was hungry." PNEUMA's "famished" is contextually understandable but adds intensity not in the verb itself. The understatement after 40 days is part of the narrative's restraint.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The text's restraint is preserved.

---

## 4:3 — "If you are the Son of God" vs. "If son you are of God" vs. "If you really are God's Son"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If son you are of God" | Preserved Greek word order (εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ θεοῦ) |
| LOGOS | "If you are the Son of God" | Standard English word order, formal |
| PNEUMA | "If you really are God's Son" | Added "really" to capture the provocative taunt |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "If you are the Son of God" — the Greek εἰ is a first-class conditional (assuming truth for argument's sake), not an expression of doubt. PNEUMA's "really" adds skepticism not in the text. EMET's word order is unnatural in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The conditional is rendered without interpretive addition.

---

## 4:4 — "It is written" vs. "It has been written" vs. "Scripture says" for Greek γέγραπται

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It has been written" | Perfect tense rendered explicitly |
| LOGOS | "It is written" | Traditional English rendering of γέγραπται |
| PNEUMA | "Scripture says" | Dynamic — personalizes the written word as an active authority |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "It is written" — the traditional rendering of the perfect passive γέγραπται. This phrase is deeply established in English biblical discourse. PNEUMA's "Scripture says" is a valid dynamic choice but changes the passive construction to active and introduces "Scripture" as a named subject not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional formula preserved throughout the temptation narrative.

---

## 4:4 — "Man shall not live by bread alone" vs. "A person does not live on bread alone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Not upon bread alone shall the man live" | Literal with inverted word order |
| LOGOS | "Man shall not live by bread alone" | Traditional KJV-influenced rendering |
| PNEUMA | "A person does not live on bread alone" | Gender-inclusive, modern idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Man shall not live by bread alone" — the Greek ἄνθρωπος is generic ("a human being") and the traditional rendering is deeply embedded in English-language culture. The formal "shall" suits the gravity of Deuteronomy quotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The traditional phrasing has become proverbial in English.

---

## 4:4 — "comes from the mouth of God" vs. "proceeds from the mouth of God" vs. "going out through the mouth of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going out through [the] mouth of God" | Literal participial rendering |
| LOGOS | "proceeds from the mouth of God" | Formal — "proceeds" for ἐκπορευομένῳ |
| PNEUMA | "comes from God's mouth" | Simplified, natural English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "comes from the mouth of God" — a compromise between LOGOS's formal "proceeds" and PNEUMA's simplified version. "Comes from" is natural English that accurately renders the Greek without being either archaic or overly casual.

**Status:** Resolved with independent phrasing. Balances formality and accessibility.

---

## 4:5 — "pinnacle of the temple" vs. "highest point of the temple" for Greek πτερύγιον τοῦ ἱεροῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the pinnacle of the temple" | Standard rendering of πτερύγιον (lit. "little wing") |
| LOGOS | "the pinnacle of the temple" | Same standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the highest point of the temple" | Descriptive rendering clarifying the meaning of πτερύγιον |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the pinnacle of the temple" — "pinnacle" is the established English term for the temple's high point and is widely understood. PNEUMA's "highest point" is clear but prosaic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Established vocabulary preferred.

---

## 4:6 — "On their hands they will bear you up" vs. "They will lift you up in their hands"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Upon hands they will bear you up" | Literal with Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "On their hands they will bear you up" | Formal English, preserving "bear up" |
| PNEUMA | "They will lift you up in their hands" | Natural English word order, "lift" for ἀροῦσιν |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "On their hands they will bear you up" preserves the fronted prepositional phrase that emphasizes the means of protection, and "bear up" is appropriate for the carrying/supporting sense of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Formal structure maintained in scriptural quotation.

---

## 4:6 — "lest you strike your foot against a stone" vs. "so you won't even stub your foot on a stone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "lest you strike against a stone the foot of you" | Literal with awkward word order |
| LOGOS | "lest you strike your foot against a stone" | Formal with "lest" |
| PNEUMA | "so you won't even stub your foot on a stone" | Colloquial — "stub" and "even" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "lest" preserves the purpose clause (μήποτε) in formal register appropriate for Scripture quotation. PNEUMA's "stub" trivializes the image and "even" is an addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Formal register in prophetic/scriptural quotation.

---

## 4:8 — "a very high mountain" vs. "an exceedingly high mountain" vs. "the peak of a very high mountain"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "an exceedingly high mountain" | ὑψηλὸν λίαν — "exceedingly" for λίαν |
| LOGOS | "a very high mountain" | Natural English with "very" for λίαν |
| PNEUMA | "the peak of a very high mountain" | Added "peak" — not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a very high mountain" — "very" naturally renders λίαν in modern English. EMET's "exceedingly" is slightly archaic. PNEUMA adds "peak" which is not in the source text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:9 — "fall down and worship me" vs. "kneel down and worship me" for Greek πεσὼν προσκυνήσῃς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having fallen down you will do-obeisance to me" | Literal with "do-obeisance" |
| LOGOS | "fall down and worship me" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "kneel down and worship me" | "Kneel" interprets πεσών (which means "fall," not "kneel") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "fall down and worship me" — πεσών (from πίπτω) means "having fallen," indicating prostration, not merely kneeling. PNEUMA's "kneel" is too mild for the full prostration demanded. As in 2:11, PNEUMA softens the physical act.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The verb πίπτω specifically means to fall/prostrate.

---

## 4:10 — "Be gone, Satan!" vs. "Go away, Satanas!" vs. "Get out of here, Satan!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Go away, Satanas!" | Literal translation of ὕπαγε with transliterated name |
| LOGOS | "Be gone, Satan!" | Formal dismissal |
| PNEUMA | "Get out of here, Satan!" | Colloquial, forceful dismissal |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Be gone, Satan!" — the Sinaiticus reads the short form Ὕπαγε Σατανᾶ (without ὀπίσω μου). "Be gone" matches the brevity and formality of the Greek imperative. PNEUMA's rendering is too colloquial for Jesus's authoritative dismissal; EMET's "Go away" is pedestrian.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Formal authority in Jesus's command.

---

## 4:11 — "ministered to him" vs. "were serving him" vs. "took care of him" for Greek διηκόνουν αὐτῷ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were serving him" | Literal rendering of imperfect διηκόνουν |
| LOGOS | "were ministering to him" | Formal — "minister" preserves theological overtone of διακονέω |
| PNEUMA | "took care of him" | Dynamic — domesticates the angelic service |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "ministered to him" — διακονέω in the context of angelic attendance carries a sense of sacred service, not mere caretaking. "Ministered" preserves this theological dimension. PNEUMA's "took care of" is too domestic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Sacred service distinguished from ordinary care.

---

## 4:12 — "arrested" vs. "delivered up" for Greek παρεδόθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "delivered up" | Literal rendering of παραδίδωμι — the same verb used for Jesus's betrayal |
| LOGOS | "arrested" | Clear English for the event described |
| PNEUMA | "arrested" | Same clear rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "arrested" — while EMET correctly notes that παραδίδωμι is the same verb later used for Jesus's betrayal/handing over, the immediate meaning here is that John was taken into custody. The verbal echo is a scholarly observation better suited to a note.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Clarity of event over verbal echo.

---

## 4:13 — "by the sea" vs. "a lakeside town" for Greek τὴν παραθαλασσίαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] by-the-sea" | Literal compound adjective |
| LOGOS | "by the sea" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a lakeside town" | Interprets θάλασσα as "lake" (which the Sea of Galilee technically is) and adds "town" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "by the sea" — Matthew calls it θάλασσα ("sea") and the translation should respect his terminology. PNEUMA's geographic correction (it is technically a lake) imposes modern categories on the ancient text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Author's own terminology preserved.

---

## 4:16 — "dwelling in darkness" vs. "sitting in darkness" vs. "sat in darkness" for Greek καθήμενος ἐν σκότει

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sitting in darkness" | Literal rendering of καθήμενος (sitting) |
| LOGOS | "dwelling in darkness" | "Dwelling" — a settled, ongoing state |
| PNEUMA | "sat in darkness" | Simple past of "sit" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "dwelling in darkness" — while the Greek participle καθήμενος literally means "sitting," in this prophetic context it describes a settled condition of existence. "Dwelling" captures the permanence of the darkness better than the physical act of sitting.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Prophetic context warrants "dwelling."

---

## 4:16 — "a great light" vs. "a blazing light" for Greek φῶς μέγα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a great light" | Literal rendering of φῶς μέγα |
| LOGOS | "a great light" | Same literal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a blazing light" | Intensified — "blazing" adds heat and violence of light |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a great light" — φῶς μέγα means "a great light," not "a blazing light." PNEUMA adds sensory specificity (heat, intensity) not in the Greek adjective μέγα, which simply means "great/large."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Adjective rendered as written.

---

## 4:17 — "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" vs. "Turn from your sins — the kingdom of heaven is near!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Repent, for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near" | Literal with plural "heavens" |
| LOGOS | "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" | Traditional formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Turn from your sins — the kingdom of heaven is near!" | Dynamic expansion of μετανοεῖτε; "near" for ἤγγικεν |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" — this echoes John's identical proclamation in 3:2. SOPHIA uses "is at hand" here (vs. "has drawn near" in 3:2) to reflect the slightly different narrative context while preserving the traditional formula. PNEUMA's "Turn from your sins" expands the single word μετανοεῖτε.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional formula maintained.

---

## 4:19 — "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men" vs. "Come after me" vs. "Come, follow me, and I will teach you to fish for people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Come after me, and I will make you fishers of men" | "Come after" for Δεῦτε ὀπίσω μου |
| LOGOS | "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Come, follow me, and I will teach you to fish for people" | "Fish for people" — gender-inclusive verb phrase; "teach" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men" — the phrase "fishers of men" is one of the most iconic in the Gospels. PNEUMA's "fish for people" updates the idiom but loses the noun-phrase construction. "Teach" is not in the Greek (ποιήσω = "I will make").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Iconic phrasing preserved.

---

## 4:23 — "good news of the kingdom" vs. "gospel of the kingdom" vs. "good news of the kingdom" for Greek εὐαγγέλιον τῆς βασιλείας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the good-news of the kingdom" | Hyphenated rendering of εὐαγγέλιον |
| LOGOS | "the gospel of the kingdom" | "Gospel" — established theological term |
| PNEUMA | "the good news of the kingdom" | "Good news" — the meaning of εὐαγγέλιον |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the good news of the kingdom" — while "gospel" is an established term, "good news" is a more transparent rendering of εὐαγγέλιον that allows the reader to hear the content of the word. This is a case where transparency serves better than tradition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA/EMET. Transparency over tradition for this term.

---

## 4:24 — "demon-oppressed" vs. "being demonized" vs. "moon-struck" vs. "those with seizures" vs. "those tormented by demons" vs. "those suffering seizures"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "those being demonized, and moon-struck [ones]" | Literal — preserves δαιμονιζομένους and σεληνιαζομένους separately |
| LOGOS | "those oppressed by demons, those with seizures" | "Oppressed" for demonized; medical term for moon-struck |
| PNEUMA | "those tormented by demons, those suffering seizures" | "Tormented" intensifies; "suffering" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the demon-oppressed, those with seizures" — for δαιμονιζομένους, "demon-oppressed" conveys the passive experience of affliction. For σεληνιαζομένους, "those with seizures" renders the recognized medical condition rather than the folk etymology ("moon-struck"). EMET's "moon-struck" preserves the etymology but is misleading in modern English. PNEUMA's "tormented" is too strong for the neutral Greek participle.

**Status:** Resolved with a blend. Each condition rendered with appropriate precision.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion or intensification | 9 occurrences | Usually rejected; Greek text is more restrained |
| EMET awkward literalism (word order, compounds) | 5 occurrences | Resolved in favor of natural English |
| LOGOS provides baseline rendering | 13 occurrences | LOGOS most often adopted or adapted |
| Register disagreement (formal vs. colloquial) | 5 occurrences | Formal register preferred, especially in Scripture quotations |
| Iconic phrase preservation | 3 occurrences | Traditional English phrasing retained (bread alone, fishers of men, Be gone Satan) |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Matthew 4 contains two distinct sections: the temptation narrative (vv.1-11) and the beginning of Jesus's Galilean ministry (vv.12-25). The temptation narrative, with its three Scripture quotations, produced the most significant disagreements, particularly around how to render the γέγραπται ("it is written") formula and the OT quotations themselves.

PNEUMA's consistent strategy of modernizing, intensifying, and expanding is most visible in this chapter: "tested" for "tempted," "famished" for "hungry," "if you really are" for "if you are," "Scripture says" for "it is written," "blazing light" for "great light." While each individual choice has a rationale, the cumulative effect distances the translation from the source text's restraint and from the established English biblical register.

The most notable resolution in favor of PNEUMA/EMET over LOGOS was the rendering of εὐαγγέλιον as "good news" rather than "gospel" — a case where transparency of meaning was judged more important than traditional ecclesiastical vocabulary.

LOGOS continued to provide the most reliable baseline, with 13 of 20 resolutions following or adapting LOGOS's rendering.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
